items.q: If 50 cm^3 of a 1 mol/dm^3 solution is diluted to 100 cm^3, what is the new concentration?
items.correct: 0.5 mol/dm^3 — the moles stay the same but the volume doubled.
items.misconception: The new concentration is 0.05 mol/dm^3 because you doubled the volume so you halve the concentration once and shift the decimal too.
items.random_wrong: The new concentration is 2 mol/dm^3 because adding water makes the solution stronger.
items.why: Students panic with unit conversions and over-divide, or they confuse 'add water = more concentrated' through a misread of the procedure.

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: Which apparatus most accurately measures 25.0 cm³ for titration?
items.why: Correct answer: Pipette. “Beaker” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The end point of a strong acid-strong base titration is best detected by:
items.why: It's Methyl orange or phenolphthalein. “Universal indicator” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
